children.text: The Panama Canal operates within fixed borders, yet its hydrological system is continuously reconfigured through technical and legal adjustments to its watershed.

In 2024, the Panama Canal Authority (ACP) announced an adjustment to the Canal watershed boundaries, expanding the spatial framework through which water is managed, monitored, and allocated. This redefinition actively reorganizes territory by incorporating new rural basins into Canal's operational domain.

The expansion of watershed limits reproduces territorial effects in advance of material infrastructure. It enables technical studies, environmental assessments, and planning processes that render projects such as the proposed Río Indio reservoir adminsitratively viable and strategically necessary.

This is not the first time in which the Canal watershed has been redefined. When the Canal was transfered from US to Panamanian control in 1999, a campesino protest movement emerged in Panama in response to a proposal that would have displaced a large number of rural communities from their land in order to create new lakes for the Panama Canal. Law 44 effectively expanded the Canal watershed by adding approximately 213,000 hectares to the existing 312,00 hectares Eastern Watershed.

title: Watershed Expansion of the Panama Canal
